blocks.text: Listen! I, Paul, am telling you that if you let yourselves be circumcised, Christ will be of no benefit to you.
Once again I testify to every man who lets himself be circumcised that he is obliged to obey the entire law.
You who want to be justified by the law have cut yourselves off from Christ; you have fallen away from grace.
For through the Spirit, by faith, we eagerly wait for the hope of righteousness.
2–5 : Paul states unequivocally that circumcision will exclude Gentiles from the Christ-community and tie them to the demands of the law, which they cannot keep (see 2.16; 3.10–12).
For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts for anything; the only thing that counts is faith working through love.

6 : , seeming to contradict the position stated in vv. 2–5, circumcision now appears to be a matter of indifference, a point that is repeated again in 6.15 (see also 1 Cor 7.19). Among the elect, the main thing is “faith made effective through love” (see textual note ). This gives trusting obedience (Gk “pistis,” ) a passive sense because it is instigated through love, presumably God's love.
You were running well; who prevented you from obeying the truth?
Such persuasion does not come from the one who calls you.
7–8 : The Galatians are deserting the true teachings of Paul. His rivals attempt to pervert the truth, which comes from , God through Christ.
A little yeast leavens the whole batch of dough.
9 : Paul uses the same saying in 1 Cor 5.6.
I am confident about you in the Lord that you will not think otherwise. But whoever it is that is confusing you will pay the penalty.
10 : Paul continues to drive a wedge between his Galatian followers and the rivals and competitors who actively seek to harm them.
But my friends, why am I still being persecuted if I am still preaching circumcision? In that case the offense of the cross has been removed.

11 : , raises a possible objection that Paul's teachings about circumcision have been inconsistent. It is unclear what role the plays in this subargument, but it suggests Paul's alienation and persecution by others (see 1 Cor 1.18–25).
I wish those who unsettle you would castrate themselves!
5.2–12: Paul's case against circumcision. A Gentile who accepts circumcision is seeking righteousness in the wrong way.
12 : Paul's polemic about his competitors comes to an apex of bitter sarcasm: his opponents should castrate themselves rather than seek to mutilate the genitals of others; see also Phil 3.2.
For you were called to freedom, brothers and sisters;b only do not use your freedom as an opportunity for selfindulgence, but through love become slaves to one another.

13 : Paul again uses freedom/slavery analogies. , lit., “the flesh,” which Paul associates negatively with self-directed desires. The alternative is enslavement to one another for the sake of love; its opposite is strife and conflict (see v. 15). Love, mutuality, and solidarity are common ethical ideals in Stoic traditions, but Paul confines them to the elect here and in Phil 2.2–4; Rom 13.8–10; 14.7–23.
For the whole law is summed up in a single commandment, “You shall love your neighbor as yourself.”

14 : Returning to the , Paul insists that the Christ-elect fulfill the law through this ethic of solidarity, here construed in terms of the command to love one's neighbor, quoting Lev 19.18 (also in Rom 13.8–10).
If, however, you bite and devour one another, take care that you are not consumed by one another.
Live by the Spirit, I say, and do not gratify the desires of the flesh.
For what the flesh desires is opposed to the Spirit, and what the Spirit desires is opposed to the flesh; for these are opposed to each other, to prevent you from doing what you want.

16–17 : The life of the “pneuma” ( ) requires alienation from (v. 13n.; 3.3; Rom 8.1–17; cf. Rom 7.14–24), which are antithetical to the “pneuma.” Greek moral philosophy considered passions and desires the root cause of immorality. Platonic discussions of self-mastery associate these irrational (bad) desires with the flesh and body, as Paul does here; see also Rom 1.24,26; 6.12–13; 7.5; 13.13–14; 1 Cor 9.24–27.
But if you are led by the Spirit, you are not subject to the law.
18 : , although v. 14 suggests that they may fulfill it in some way.
Now the works of the flesh are obvious: fornication, impurity, licentiousness,
idolatry, sorcery, enmities, strife, jealousy, anger, quarrels, dissensions, factions,
envy, drunkenness, carousing, and things like these. I am warning you, as I warned you before: those who do such things will not inherit the kingdom of God.
By contrast, the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, generosity, faithfulness,
gentleness, and self-control. There is no law against such things.

19–23 : Catalogues of vices and virtues were commonly used in Greco-Roman ethical instruction and were adapted by early Christian writers; see Rom 1.29–31; 1 Cor 6.9–10; 2 Cor 6.6–7; 12.20–21; in other early Christian literature, see Col 3.5–14; Mk 7.21–23; Mt 15.19; 1 Tim 1.9–10; 6.11; 2 Tim 3.2–5; 1 Pet 2.1; 4.3; 2 Pet 1.5–7; Rev 21.8; 22.15. Paul's virtue and vice lists are relatively conventional but he adds language about idolatry, the kingdom of God, the law, the “pneuma” (“Spirit”), and belonging to Christ.
And those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires.

24 : The use of crucifixion as an analogy for controlling the passions is distinctive. Paul elsewhere uses death metaphors to convey domination and control (7.7–25), as does Philo (e.g., 1.105–107; 2.78; 73).
If we live by the Spirit, let us also be guided by the Spirit.
Let us not become conceited, competing against one another, envying one another.
5.13–6.10: Ethics among the Christ-elect. In a shift of topic and tone, Paul presents a series of moral teachings that center on mutuality and solidarity.
5.13–26: The constraints on freedom among the elect.
